---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## Calibrating the Verb and Positioning the Literature

- Part 4 — calibrate the verb, position the literature review

::: notes
Welcome back to Writing the Empirical Paper, part four of nine. In part three we built the one-sentence contribution and named its job. Now we calibrate the verb in that sentence to the strength of the evidence, stress-test it, and then turn to the section most student papers get wrong: the literature review that positions your work and makes the gap visible.
:::

## Calibrate the Verb, Then Stress-Test

- **Strongest verb bounded by weakest assumption**
- Clean staggered DiD, flat pre-trends → "credibly estimate"
- OLS with a plausible omitted variable → "robust association"
- Never "show that X causes Y" without the design
- Hand it to a classmate; if they ask "is that causal?", rewrite

::: notes
Calibrate the verb to the evidence — this is the causal-language discipline, previewed here because it bites first in this sentence. If your design is a clean staggered difference-in-differences with flat pre-trends, you have earned "credibly estimate." If your design is ordinary least squares with a plausible omitted variable you could not rule out, you have not, and your sentence says "document a robust association" or "provide suggestive evidence" — never "show that X causes Y." The reading guides of Week Five trained you to catch authors who overclaim in exactly this sentence; the grader of your paper runs the same check. A practical test: hand your one-sentence contribution to a classmate who has not seen your project and ask them to tell you, from that sentence alone, what you did and why it matters. If they ask "wait, what's the question?" or "is that causal?", the sentence is doing the introduction's whole job badly.
:::

## The Annotated-Bibliography Trap

- Failure mode has a name: the **annotated bibliography**
- "Smith finds A. Jones finds B. We contribute." — a **list**
- It tells what exists, not **where the hole is**
- One job: draw a **map** so the gap shows as a gap
- Build by **grouping and contrasting**, not enumerating

::: notes
Now the literature review, where most student papers go wrong. The failure has a name: the annotated bibliography. That is the review that reads "Smith finds A, Jones finds B, Garcia finds C, we contribute to this literature." It is a list. It tells the reader what exists but not where the hole is, and the hole is the only thing the section exists to reveal. A literature review does exactly one job: it draws a map of what is known so that the gap you fill is visible as a gap. You build that map by grouping and contrasting, not by enumerating. Cluster the prior work into two or three strands and characterize each by what it established and what it could not. One strand documents the raw denial gap but cannot separate treatment from applicant composition; a second uses audit studies to isolate treatment but cannot speak to the population-scale effect of policy; we sit at the intersection. Now the gap is not asserted — it is constructed, brick by brick, out of the literature itself.
:::

## Three Rules to Keep It Honest

- **Load-bearing:** if a citation can be deleted, delete it
- **Generous and precise** about limits, never dismissive
- "Prior work was wrong" is the voice of the unread
- **Position, do not summarize** — one sentence per paper
- Verify citations; a hallucinated reference is unforgivable

::: notes
Three rules keep the section honest. First, every paper you cite should be load-bearing — it either builds part of the map's wall or marks the edge of the hole. If a citation could be deleted without changing where the gap appears, delete it; a padded review signals you are counting references rather than building an argument. Second, be generous to the work you build on and precise about its limits, never dismissive. "Prior work was wrong" is the voice of someone about to be refereed by the author they just insulted. "Prior work established X under conditions Y; we extend to where Y fails" is the voice of someone who belongs in the conversation. Third, position, do not summarize — the reader needs each paper's coordinate on your map, one sentence per paper. And always verify your citations; a hallucinated reference is the one error a referee can catch in ten seconds and never forgive.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Calibrate the verb to your **weakest assumption** and stress-test the sentence on a classmate. The literature review draws a **map** so the gap shows as a gap — group and contrast, keep every citation load-bearing, position rather than summarize. Next: data and design.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part four: calibrate the verb in your contribution sentence to your weakest assumption, and stress-test the sentence on a classmate. The literature review does one job — it draws a map so the gap is visible as a gap — built by grouping and contrasting, not enumerating. Keep every citation load-bearing, position rather than summarize, and never fabricate a reference. In part five we enter the body of the paper — the data section and the design section.
:::
